FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, baseline 9-10 year-olds, 21 US research sites, ABCD 2.0.1 data release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis comparing video gamers vs non-video gamers on cognitive task performance and fMRI BOLD signal.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open; no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The only gaming variable is total video gaming hours per week derived from the item 'Play video games on a computer, console, phone, or other devices' — an unspecified-platform aggregate (computer + console + phone + other). No separate phone-based gaming coefficient exists anywhere in the paper. Step 2 not reached. Per binding clarification, generic unspecified-platform gaming fails FT-IC2 unless paired with a separately measured phone/SM variable, which is absent here.

notes: Paper studies video gaming (unspecified platform: computer, console, phone, other devices combined) and cognitive/neural outcomes. Gaming variable does not isolate phone-based gaming and no separate phone/SM variable is analyzed. Excluded under FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, 21 research sites, ABCD 3.0 data release; participants aged 11-14 at COVID-19 survey.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ML analysis (XGBoost/logistic regression) predicting pandemic mental health outcomes from prepandemic and pandemic-period predictors.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article; no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Figure 4B (depressive symptoms model) lists 'texting on a cell phone, tablet, or computer' with SHAP value 0.07 as a predictor of depressive symptoms — a qualifying phone/SM variable (texting) paired with an individual-trait outcome (depressive symptoms). Additional screen time features with SHAP values appear across models (social media time, online communication frequency). At least one phone/SM-specific estimate is paired with an individual-trait variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: SHAP values (mean absolute) reported for all features including 'texting on a cell phone, tablet, or computer' (SHAP 0.07) predicting depressive symptoms; AUC metrics reported for all four classification models (mean AUC >= 0.70).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential ML analysis with quantitative SHAP feature importance values and AUC performance metrics reported throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time/texting variables serve as IVs (predictors of mental health outcomes); mental health is the DV. FT-EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis, which is not the case here.
notes: ML classification study predicting pandemic mental health (anxiety, depression, stress, positive affect) from multiple predictors including screen time features. Texting and social media time appear as IVs with SHAP-quantified associations with mental health outcomes. Cross-sectional (same-survey) and longitudinal (prepandemic-to-pandemic) components both present.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study wave 1, n=6,758, 21 US sites, baseline age ~9.94 years.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical latent profile analysis (LPA) of fMRI data to identify neurodevelopmental subgroups.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article; no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Screen time is the only screen-related variable analyzed. Its label 'screen time' is not phone/SM-specific, so Step 1 finds no phone/SM-specific estimate. Step 2: Per the supplement methods, the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey composite includes time spent on TV shows/movies, YouTube videos, video games (computer/console/phone/other devices), texting, social networking, and video chatting — a bundle of phone/SM and non-phone items (TV, console gaming, PC gaming, unspecified-platform gaming) with no separate phone/SM breakout reported in any analysis. Supplemental Table S3 reports screen time as a single composite variable (F=6.80, p<.0001) across neurodevelopmental profiles without any per-modality breakdown. Per Step 2, composite fails because it includes TV, console/PC gaming, and other non-phone items without a separate breakout.

notes: LPA study identifying 7 neurodevelopmental profiles from fMRI data; screen time (total composite bundling TV, gaming, texting, SM, video chat) compared across profiles in exploratory analyses. No phone/SM-specific variable isolated. Excluded under FT-EC2.
